speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your work with the joint distribution--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually we were talking about the fate of your family members. Chaskl, You said had died in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Chuma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chuma has survived, and she's in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain at the DP camp at Belsen, until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1948, till we immigrated to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say we, who are you referring to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because my brother came before. He came January 16, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your meeting of your wife in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm. That was exactly the day I came back from Paris, and I met her near the swimming pool. And so it did go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- obviously, this was in DP camp in Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct

speaker: interviewer
text: So you dated her during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what the DP camp was like. Were there social activities? Were there cultural activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were social activities. There were also like sport and schools for children. And were theaters, movies. There actually was one building, but they tried-- so they joined the Jewish agency, they tried to provide us with activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were dating your future wife while you were in Belsen in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: What things would you do together? Oh, well, sometime we went like to an opera, we went to dance, being in the Bergen-Belsen, because it was organized there. It was-- it was a good life, and it was not so good, because people didn't know what to do. A lot of people did wait to immigrate. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's all. Where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in Celle in Germany in the City Hall. And then we came over here. We had the another [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not with the people but just we went to the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there some reason you weren't married in Belsen in the DP camp there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no reason. The only thing that had come out the immigration so fast. So we-- because my wife had papers to go to Sweden. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she fell in love with me, and I fell in love with her. And we had to make it fast, because we had to give it to the immigration, the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the date of your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June the 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you then immigrated to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Canada, Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the first-- I remember at night was Rosh Hashanah. I think it was the 1st of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or September or November, something like--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948. 1st of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your arrival to Canada. What city did you come to and what was it like establishing yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to Halifax. We came by the boat, General [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from Halifax we came to Toronto. And my brother, he did wait on the station for us. That's all. We very happy that we are in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do when you came to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailoring. I started to work on [INAUDIBLE] at the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children, Mr. Jacobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally and Tomy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Sally was born when?

speaker: interviewer
text: And she is married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, happily married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bentley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have two grandchildren, Jessica and Shoshanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Tomy was born when?

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your occupation in Canada always been a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you currently still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working now or are you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm still working a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you involved in any organizations in Canada? B'nai B'rith or any Landsmannschaft organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsmannschaft, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Tomaszow Society.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have contacts still with people from Tomaszow here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, with people from Tomaszow, yeah, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were witness after the war at some war crimes trials, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whose trials were you a witness and at whose behest? Who requested that you be a witness at these trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first trial was in 1970. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was near Frankfurt, a suburb from Frankfurt. I forgot the city. The trial was against Fuchs, Boettich and Reichler. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The was the first trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boettich was a Gestapo. Fuchs and Reichler, they were SS. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Boettich was a volksdeutsche. That means a Polish-German. Fuchs was also a volksdeutsche Polish-German, and Reichler was from Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you have contact with them during the war? Where was it that you had Witnessed their--

speaker: interviewee
text: The contact-- I didn't have a special contact, but I did see him, and I did see what they did, the Reichler and Fuchs, they were the main killers of Jews-- the main.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see them do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just take the gun and killed, just like this. That Fuchs was-- everybody, not only me, everybody did see it. They didn't hide from anybody. In front of your eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Reichler had a partner too. His name was Olch. I didn't see him on the trial. All of these walked around with two dogs. Ooh.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who had requested that you be a witness at those trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- German government. They did come here to look for witnesses, not only me, a lot of people from Tomaszow, the went on trial-- on this trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the result of that trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as I know, they did get some kind of sentence. I don't know how many years or whatever it is. But Fuchs and Reichler, they got sentence. Boettich, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't get such a big sentence, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe those men as murderers?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, the Fuchs and Reichler? For sure. Boettich? Boettich, I knew, because when we did play soccer, he did play first in the Victoria German team, and then he did play at the [INAUDIBLE]. So that's where I knew him. He knew me too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were a witness in another trial as well, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've been in Hamburg twice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the lagerfuhrer from Blizyn. His name is Nell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And apparently he got life sentence. The only thing I remember, he didn't talk at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But during the trial, I asked the judge through a dolmetscher-- interpreter-- or I can ask him a question, and they, sure, any question you want, you can ask him. I ask him, did anybody in the camp receive a fair trial like he did-- like you do? There was no comment. He didn't-- not a word.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't speak not a word during the whole trial. He was-- before he went into the courtroom, he was free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I recognized him right away. I said to my wife, I said, that's him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it that you had witnessed him doing? During his time as lagerfuhrer, what was it that you had witnessed or were you there just to provide identification that that was him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, they brought over pictures here, were, you know, some judges and the prosecutors from the German court. And what I witnessed by him, that he was-- I didn't see him killing himself, but he did give orders. I remember two people from Piotrkow, they did escape Blizyn, and they catch them. And they made an appeal, and everybody had to watch the way they've been shot. So two Ukrainians, they shot. They could have done white gloves, and they had to knee, and they went over right here and just like this. One, as even on the trial, they ask me. I remember even the name from the Ukraine. One-- one was Moskolenko. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he gave the orders. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He gave the orders, not only him, to shoot some other people too.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you had come to Toronto, once you had started settling in Toronto and rebuilding your life, what was the reception of the Jewish community in Toronto-- not the European, not the survivor community-- but the community in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to my opinion, very, very nice. Very, very nice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't say anything about it. Maybe some people, which always they were not so happy. We were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why have you chosen to speak in public about your experiences, to allow for this interview, is what I'm asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it's very, very important for the future generations and probably for my grandchildren, as one time my granddaughter asked me, Zayde, what is that number? She said, it's your phone number? And I didn't talk to them about this. But later on, they talked to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I answered them, special last year. We were in Poland. I took my son-in-law, my granddaughter to Birkenau, and they did see, they witnessed, which they wanted to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's important for them to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going? What sustained you during the time that you were in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say nothing, because we live. We didn't think of the future. We didn't know what's going to be tomorrow. You live day by day, hour by hour. The only thing we knew, one of these days will be finished. This we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a reason for your survival or was it just plain luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plain luck. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's what I think, plain luck. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't do any miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever during the war subject to acts of kindness? During the war when you were in the camps, during that period in the ghetto, were you ever subject to acts of kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kindness to each other?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Or for from Poles or anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always-- I am that kind of person. But sometimes it's no good, the way I am. I know myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have experiences, though, with the gentile community or German guards or anybody that was kind towards you during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this-- yeah, I one Polish guy, yes. He's in Texas now. As a matter fact, he is from Warsaw. And he was always with us after the liberation. I remember when I went to look for my sister, we went to Poland. He went too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went to Warsaw, and he was stitched to be anti-Semitic. He told us. His name, Wacek Malinowski. And he went to Warsaw. He had a sister and a mother in Warsaw. And he said, how could you do a thing to me like this and teach me against Jews? I live with Jews. My name is David, and the whole families and-- that's what he-- and he said, I'm going. I don't want to come anymore. And it's a matter of fact, he married a Jewish girl too. And where was it that you had contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ohrdruf. He was a cook, and I was a cook. And he was the guy, after they threw out all the Jews, he helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would give me bread every day, soup. Everybody who did work in the kitchen had-- had a chance to do this-- if he wants to. Have you had contact with him since the end of the war? We've been all the time, all the years in Bergen-Belsen, and since he went to Texas, I never been in contact with him. I don't know why, and I ask so many times-- many, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: You asked what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, he passed away. So one friend told me, but he lives in Texas-- in Houston, TX.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your faith before the war, your religious faith before the war as being changed in any way after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't change. I'm the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in my faith. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not at all. I believe I'm Jewish. I'm proud to be Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do. The first thing something you say, oh, my god.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. That's the way I was brought up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you say the first thing somebody would think would be oh, my god.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you saying that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you always think God helps you-- you always think. Or it's true or it's not true, but that's the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: By surviving? I don't know. I would say it's luck. I have no idea how to describe this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that there's any meaning in the Holocaust for the Jewish people or for the world at large? Is there any meaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the Holocaust for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jews and for the rest of the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They should know, which a lot of people, they learn, and they knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing, I hope it would never ever happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have dreams about the war after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of dreams? What would happen in your dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: That somebody go after me, the same Meister Pichler, with the gun. He wants to kill me, and I escape, and I start to scream, and I scream. and my wife, she came up, she slapped me in the face, oh, a few times. If she would be here, she would tell you. And apparently such a terrible scream. It's not a normal screen, she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: A long time I didn't have any now. But still sometime. Oh, did I have those dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother, Nojech, whom recently passed away, and your sister, Chuma, is still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever with them talk about life in Tomaszow before the war about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime. Sometime. But my brother, he passed away, but my sister, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she can't hear about Poland. She-- her husband, they went away to Europe. Her husband is from Warsaw. He said let's go. She said for no money in the world. You just put down $100 million, she wouldn't go. She wouldn't go. Do you think your survival had anything to do with the fact that you and your brother were together? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we did stick together, as plain as this. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make you stronger to have your brother with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you speak to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember he stares, sometime would come in his eyes, when I did work in the kitchen in Ohrdruf, and he did come over after the shift and look at me into my room and the way I looked. I looked so nice and not nourished and so you understand. And he did-- he was the happiest man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very good. The relationship was very good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before the war and after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that your experiences during the war have interfered with your life after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. No, not at. I'm the-- I'm the same person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences had any effect on your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No. No, because, to be honest with you, we didn't talk so much. But lately, we've talked about. In the beginning, we didn't. Maybe it wasn't right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that lately you've begun to talk about it more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they start to growing, and they went to school, and they are learning something. So that's the reason they ask me questions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like the little one, Shoshanah, said Zayde, when you were in camp, what did you do? I said I had to work. And she said, if you didn't want to work. I said they could shoot me. They could kill me. And you know what she mentioned to me? She said I rather they shoot me and I go to heaven [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and not to do what they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is Shoshanah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, 11 years, she will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we conclude the interview, Mr. Jacobs, is there any message that you would like to make to leave for future generations, any statement at all in relation to your experiences that you would like to make for posterity for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to. The only thing is my wish [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS] that my grandchildren and all the children in the world that don't have to go through that experience what I went through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And be good to each other. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] That's a [INAUDIBLE]. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Jacobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
